messages.content: write the topicality, object, hypothesis, objective of the work "The work of verbal noun over the last 200 years in the English language"

Topicality: The evolution and usage of verbal nouns in the English language over the past 200 years.

Object: To analyze and explore the changes in the use of verbal nouns in English over the last two centuries.

Hypothesis: The hypothesis of the work could be to assess whether there has been a significant shift in the frequency, types, or patterns of verbal nouns in the English language over the past 200 years.

Objective: The objective of the work is to provide a comprehensive study of the historical development and usage of verbal nouns in the English language, including their prevalence, linguistic features, and potential changes in their usage over time. This research aims to contribute to our understanding of the evolution of the English language and shed light on the factors that have influenced the use of verbal nouns over the past two centuries.
write the topicality, object, hypothesis, objective of the work “The work of verbal noun over the last 200 years in the English language” in 350 words

Title: The Work of Verbal Nouns over the Last 200 Years in the English Language

Topicality:
The study of how verbal nouns have evolved in the English language over the last two centuries is of great topical interest. Understanding the changes in usage, frequency, and patterns of verbal nouns can shed light on the development of the English language over time.

Object:
The objective of this work is to analyze and investigate the historical usage and evolution of verbal nouns in the English language over the past 200 years. By examining various linguistic data and sources, the aim is to provide a comprehensive understanding of how verbal nouns have changed and adapted in this time period.

Hypothesis:
It is hypothesized that there may have been significant shifts in the frequency, types, and patterns of verbal nouns in the English language over the last two centuries. Specifically, there might be an increase or decrease in certain types of verbal nouns, alterations in their grammatical functions, or changes in their relative importance in different contexts.

Objective:
The primary objective of this work is to conduct a thorough analysis of the work of verbal nouns in the English language over the last 200 years. This includes:

1. Examining linguistic sources such as dictionaries, grammars, and corpora to collect data on verbal nouns used over the time period in question.
2. Identifying and categorizing different types of verbal nouns, such as gerunds, infinitives, and participles, and examining their frequency and usage.
3. Investigating any grammatical changes in the use of verbal nouns, such as shifts in their syntactical positions, collocations, or patterns of noun-to-verb conversion.
4. Exploring potential shifts in the semantic properties and functions of verbal nouns, considering both their referential and non-referential aspects.
5. Assessing the impact of sociocultural and historical factors on the usage and prevalence of verbal nouns, such as changes in technological advancements, social norms, or educational trends.
6. Comparing the findings with existing studies and theories on the evolution of the English language to contribute to the wider understanding of language change.

By achieving these objectives, this work aims to provide a comprehensive study of the historical development and usage of verbal nouns in the English language. Ultimately, it seeks to deepen our understanding of how language evolves over time and the factors that influence such changes.

Write a short description of the chapter "All-encompassing observation of the verbal noun"

Chapter: All-Encompassing Observation of the Verbal Noun

In this chapter, we dive deep into an all-encompassing observation of the verbal noun, taking a comprehensive approach to understand its usage, forms, and functions. The aim is to provide a holistic view of the verbal noun and highlight its significance in the English language.

Drawing on extensive research and linguistic analysis, this chapter examines a wide range of examples and sources to explore the different aspects of the verbal noun. We delve into its various forms, such as gerunds, infinitives, and participles, and explore their syntactic and semantic properties. The chapter also investigates how the verbal noun has evolved over time, considering changes in usage, frequency, and patterns.

Furthermore, we take into account sociolinguistic factors that can influence the usage of the verbal noun in different contexts. We examine how certain professions, social groups, or cultural factors may shape the prevalence and importance of verbal nouns. The chapter also explores potential differences in the use of verbal nouns across different English-speaking regions or time periods.

Additionally, this chapter considers the role of verbal nouns in various linguistic phenomena, such as noun-to-verb conversion, collocations, and phrase structures. By examining these aspects, we aim to provide a thorough and multifaceted understanding of the verbal noun and its significance in the English language.

Overall, this chapter presents an all-encompassing observation of the verbal noun, drawing upon extensive research and analysis from linguistic sources. By examining its forms, functions, and usage patterns, we provide valuable insights into the nature and evolution of the verbal noun in the English language. This chapter contributes to the broader understanding of language change and the complex dynamics of language usage over time.

rephrase the sentences using other words "The verbal noun denotes an object, often seen as the result of some kind of
activity: (a) reading, (a) knocking; an occupation, a sphere of activity."
The verbal noun represents an entity, frequently perceived as the outcome of a particular action: (an) act of reading, (an) act of knocking; a profession, a domain of engagement.

rephrase the text using other words "It should be noticed that nominalization takes place in the verbal noun, too. In this situation, nominalization preserves certain properties of the name: the category of definiteness (speaking about articles and pronouns placing before the verbal noun). This nominalization acquires the morpho-syntactic properties of the verbal noun, losing certain properties of the original verb. The order and probability of acquiring / losing various properties during nominalization is discussed in typological works"

It is worth observing that nominalization also occurs within the verbal noun. In such cases, nominalization retains specific characteristics of a noun, such as definiteness (referring to the placement of articles and pronouns before the verbal noun). This process of nominalization adopts the morpho-syntactic features associated with the verbal noun while relinquishing certain properties of the original verb. The sequence and likelihood of acquiring or relinquishing different properties during nominalization are addressed and examined in typological literature.
rephrase the sentence using other words “Here, nominalization doesn’t preserve order and voice indicators"
In this context, the process of nominalization does not retain the indicators of order and voice.
rephrase the sentence using other words "the morphology of the verbal noun"
The morphological structure of the verbal noun.
rephrase the sentence using other words "According to its morphological characteristics, the verbal noun possesses the grammatical category of number"
Based on its morphological attributes, the verbal noun exhibits the grammatical feature of number.

rephrase the text using other words  "This category expresses the quantitative relations that exist in reality, reflected in the minds of the speakers of a given language and having
morphological expression in the corresponding forms of the language. The category of number is represented by means of singularity and plurality, which find their expression in singular and plural forms. To form a plural, we have to add the inflection -s at the end of the word."

This linguistic category conveys the quantitative relationships present in the real world, perceived by speakers of a particular language and manifested through the morphological forms in that language. The category of number is manifested through singular and plural forms, representing the notions of singularity and plurality respectively. To indicate a plural form, the inflection -s must be appended at the end of the word.

It is important to note that there exist certain categories of nouns that deviate from the typical patterns of number. These nouns possess only one form, either singular or plural. Nouns that exclusively have a singular form are referred to as singularia tantum, while those that exclusively have a plural form are known as pluralia tantum.

As for singularia tantum verbal nouns, examples include thinking and messing. These nouns do not possess a plural counterpart. On the other hand, pluralia tantum verbal nouns, such as savings and belongings, solely exist in the plural form.
What verbal nouns with the ending -ing- do not have a plural? Give 10 examples.

Verbal nouns with the ending "-ing" typically do not have a plural form, as they are already derived from verbs. Here are 10 examples of verbal nouns with the ending "-ing" that do not have a plural:

1. Singing
2. Dancing
3. Running
4. Reading
5. Writing
6. Cooking
7. Swimming
8. Painting
9. Sleeping
10. Thinking

These examples remain unchanged in both singular and plural usage.
What pluralia tantum verbal nouns with the ending -ing-. Give examples

Pluralia tantum verbal nouns are those that only exist in the plural form. However, verbal nouns with the ending "-ing" usually do not fall under the category of pluralia tantum. Most verbal nouns with the "-ing" ending can express both singular and plural concepts. Nevertheless, here are a few examples of nouns that are commonly used in the plural form:

1. Savings
2. Proceedings
3. Proceeds
4. Earnings
5. Drawings
6. Warnings
7. Offerings
8. Findings
9. Dealings
10. Doings

While these nouns are often used in the plural, it is important to note that they can also be used in the singular form depending on the context.
What singularia tantum verbal nouns with the ending -ing-. Give examples

Singularia tantum verbal nouns are those that exist exclusively in the singular form. However, verbal nouns with the ending "-ing" are typically not considered singularia tantum. Most verbal nouns ending in "-ing" can be used in both the singular and plural forms. Nevertheless, here are a few examples of nouns that are commonly used only in the singular form:

1. News (as in "The news is interesting.")
2. Scenery (as in "The scenery is breathtaking.")
3. Feedback (as in "I appreciate your feedback.")
4. Equipment (as in "The equipment is in good condition.")
5. Baggage (as in "Her baggage is missing.")
6. Garbage (as in "Take out the garbage.")
7. Furniture (as in "The furniture is well-crafted.")
8. Knowledge (as in "Knowledge is power.")
9. Luggage (as in "He left his luggage at the airport.")
10. Traffic (as in "The traffic is heavy.")

While these nouns are typically treated as singular, it is worth noting that they can sometimes be used metaphorically or in certain contexts to convey a plural meaning.

rephrase the text using other words "Like any noun, a verbal noun can be described in terms of combinability. Speaking about syntactic units, a noun can be used by any parts of sentence other than a simple verbal predicate. From the point of view of various parts of speech, the noun is combined with an adjective, verb, pronoun, numeral, articles, participles. Also, nouns can be consistent with prepositions ."

Similar to any noun, the combinational properties of a verbal noun can be examined. When considering syntactic structures, a noun can function in different roles within a sentence, excluding the role of a main verb predicate. The noun can interact with various parts of speech, including adjectives, verbs, pronouns, numerals, articles, and participles. Additionally, nouns can also be used in conjunction with prepositions.

rephrase the text using other words "Just like any noun, the verbal noun falls under the category of definiteness. Thus, it can be preceded by either a definite or an indefinite article or demonstrative pronouns. The definite article has the functions of individualization and identification of the noun. It can show that the thing is manifested in a “remarkable” way or it’s known to the speaker as well. Briefly, it has the specifying function ( to highlight a particular object which is different from other objects), generic function (to refer to a class of objects)

Similar to all nouns, the verbal noun belongs to the category of definiteness, and as such, it can be preceded by either a definite or an indefinite article or demonstrative pronouns. The definite article, in particular, serves various functions, including individualization and identification of the noun. It indicates that the thing being referred to is notable or known by the speaker. In short, the definite article serves the purpose of specification by emphasizing a specific object that distinguishes it from others, as well as a generic function by referring to a class of objects.

rephrase the text using other words "The indefinite article doesn’t possess the indication of “uniqueness”. Its combination with a noun places the name of an object as unknown to the speaker. The indefinite article possess a nominating function (it is used to name an object), a generalizing function (to express a member of the class), a numeric function (it indicates “oneness” of objects), an aspective function (identify a certain aspect of notions)"

The absence of the indication of "uniqueness" characterizes the use of the indefinite article. When combined with a noun, the indefinite article signifies the object as unfamiliar or unknown to the speaker. The indefinite article serves several functions, including nominating objects, generalizing to express membership within a class, indicating singularity of objects, and identifying specific aspects of notions.

rephrase the text using other words "Just like any noun, the verbal noun falls under the category of definiteness. Thus, it can be preceded by either a definite or an indefinite article or demonstrative pronouns. The definite article has the functions of individualization and identification of the noun. It can show that the thing is manifested in a “remarkable” way or it’s known to the speaker as well. Briefly, it has the specifying function ( to highlight a particular object which is different from other objects), generic function (to refer to a class of objects)  The indefinite article doesn’t possess the indication of “uniqueness”. Its combination with a noun places the name of an object as unknown to the speaker. The indefinite article possess a nominating function (it is used to name an object), a generalizing function (to express a member of the class), a numeric function (it indicates “oneness” of objects), an aspective function (identify a certain aspect of notions)”

Similar to all nouns, the verbal noun is categorized by its level of definiteness. Consequently, it can be preceded by a definite article, an indefinite article, or demonstrative pronouns. The definite article serves the purpose of individualizing and identifying the noun. It signifies that the object is remarkable or known to the speaker. In essence, the definite article performs two key functions: specification, which highlights a specific object that distinguishes it from others, and generalization, which denotes a class of objects.

On the other hand, the indefinite article lacks the notion of "uniqueness". When used in conjunction with a noun, it suggests that the object is unknown or unfamiliar to the speaker. The indefinite article carries distinctive functions such as nomination, as it is used to name an object, general
